tagged_story_for_tts:
Nuclear furnace. Earth’s timekeeper.

The name sun comes from Old English sunne, tied to the Proto-Germanic sunnǭ—a word that simply meant "the shining one." It’s a label earned by brute force: a plasma sphere so hot its surface glows white, while its core fuses 600 million tons of hydrogen into helium every second.

The sun doesn’t burn like wood. It crushes. Gravity squeezes hydrogen atoms until they merge, releasing energy in a chain reaction that’s been running for 4.6 billion years—like a battery that never drains, only slows. Light from this reaction takes 10,000–170,000 years to claw from core to surface, bouncing between particles in a photon traffic jam. Once free, it races to Earth in 8 minutes.

Without the sun’s magnetic grip, solar wind—a storm of charged particles—would strip Earth’s atmosphere like sandblasting. Instead, the sun’s field loops and snaps, flinging bursts of radiation that paint auroras at the poles or, in 1859, fried telegraph wires worldwide. Plants hijack its light to split water molecules, storing energy as sugar—the trick that feeds nearly all life.

Three related subjects are solar storms’ tech risks, how stars die differently, and photosynthesis as Earth’s hack.
